romania.title: Acţionarii Romgaz au aprobat preluarea Azomureş. Conducerea, mandatată să realizeze formalităţile pentru finalizarea tranzacţiei
romania.summary: Acţionarii Romgaz au aprobat, luni, preluarea combinatului Azomureş Târgu-Mureş, acordând mandat conducerii societăţii pentru a realiza toate formalităţile necesare pentru finalizarea tranzacţiei.

Potrivit unor surse din interior, încă un procuror proaspăt angajat a „comis-o” şi a ajuns de râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis. „A sărit ca ars! Să fi văzut ce faţă a făcut! Vaiii, trebuia să-l filmez”, a mai transmis, printre […] The post Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: În timp ce presa vuieşte despre cei doi turişti ciupiţi de peştele iepure pe insulele greceşti, o creatură mult mai agresivă a pus stăpânire pe litoralul nostru! E vorba despre peştele boxă cu dinţi albaştri, cunoscut şi ca jebeleul de mare sau stavridul cu baterie, o creatură acvatică invazivă, extrem de insistentă şi fără prădători […] The post Peştele iepure e mic copil! romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
romania.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Biomarker-guided care to improve antidepressant response rates and reduce trial-and-error prescribing
medical.summary: For millions of people living with depression, finding an effective treatment can feel like a long and uncertain journey. Patients often spend months trying different medications, enduring side effects and persistent symptoms while waiting to discover whether a prescription will help. A new study led by researchers at the University of California, Irvine, and Mass General Brigham-affiliated McLean Hospital suggests there may be a better way.

medical.title: Why the first trimester of a pregnancy could be vital in driving human brain evolution
medical.summary: A new study has examined how mothers influence the size of their child's head—and, as a result, brain size and future IQ—during the first trimester of pregnancy.

medical.title: Study reveals a distinct subtype of eosinophilic esophagitis
medical.summary: Researchers from Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago identified a distinct subtype of eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)—a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder in which the esophagus narrows, interfering with food passing properly. They found that 25% of children with EoE have hypermobile joints, meaning their joints can bend beyond the normal range of motion. These patients also tend to experience autonomic symptoms, most commonly chronic lightheadedness upon standing. This combination of symptoms leads to a significantly worse quality of life, according to the study published in the Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology.

medical.title: Scientists discover why exercise reverses muscle aging
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered a molecular “switch” that helps explain why exercise keeps ageing muscles healthy. By reducing levels of a gene called DEAF1, physical activity allows older muscles to clear out damage, repair themselves, and maintain strength.

medical.title: Researchers discover treatable cause of severe anemia associated with a cancer therapy
medical.summary: Researchers at the Mount Sinai Tisch Cancer Center have identified a previously underrecognized folate deficiency that may cause severe anemia in some patients receiving PARP inhibitors, a widely used class of targeted cancer therapies. Their findings, published in the latest issue of the journal Blood Red Cells & Iron, suggest that routine screening and treatment with folic acid could help many patients avoid blood transfusions, hospitalizations and interruptions to their cancer treatment.

medical.title: Scientists solve a 30-year rye pollen mystery that could transform cancer research
medical.summary: Scientists have finally solved a nearly 30-year-old mystery surrounding two unusual molecules found in rye pollen that once showed an intriguing ability to help animals fight tumors. By determining their exact 3D structures, researchers have unlocked the blueprint needed to investigate how these natural compounds interact with the immune system and which parts may be responsible for their cancer-fighting effects.

medical.title: Men may be more likely than women to receive later-stage diagnoses for many cancer types in the US
medical.summary: Between 2015 and 2022, men were more likely than women to be diagnosed with regional and/or distant stages of 20 nonreproductive solid cancer types in the United States, according to a study published in Cancer Epidemiology, Biomarkers & Prevention, a journal of the American Association for Cancer Research (AACR).

medical.title: Researchers discover molecular 'switch' that simultaneously manages touch and pain
medical.summary: A research team led by Prof. Hyosang Lee of the Department of Brain Sciences at DGIST has identified TWIK-1, a type of potassium channel expressed in the spinal cord and peripheral sensory neurons of the somatosensory system, as a key molecular regulator of touch sensation and chronic pain. The study is significant because it presents a new molecular mechanism in the spinal cord that regulates touch responses and reveals the cause of persistent neuropathic pain, an intractable disorder, as well as a therapeutic target. The paper is published in the journal Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy.

medical.title: Brain tumor vaccine links mutation targeting to eight-year survival gains
medical.summary: A novel vaccination strategy against certain malignant brain tumors could fundamentally improve treatment for patients. Researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ), Mannheim University Medical Center, Heidelberg University Hospital and numerous partner institutions have published encouraging long-term results from a clinical trial involving a vaccine that activates the immune system against a common genetic mutation in these tumors.

medical.title: Violence may bring menopause up to 20 months earlier, review suggests
medical.summary: A study by the Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology at the University of Granada has revealed that exposure to violence against women throughout life has lasting effects that extend into middle age. The research, published in Maturitas, shows that women who have experienced violence suffer more severe menopausal symptoms and may reach menopause up to 20 months earlier than those without such a history. The study notes that for every woman who dies as a result of violence, more than 400 suffer serious disabilities, the aftereffects of which are often confused with or exacerbated by menopause.

medical.title: Social media affects teen dental confidence
medical.summary: Using social media can have a negative impact on adolescents' confidence about the appearance of their teeth, new research shows. University of Otago–Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka researchers have co-authored a paper, published in Health Marketing Quarterly, that recommends policymakers move away from idealized smile imagery in oral health marketing.

medical.title: Paying stores to curb tobacco, alcohol and lottery ads shows early promise
medical.summary: An initiative focused on partnering with retailers to decrease the influence of tobacco, alcohol and lottery marketing is showing promise and could present a more effective way to protect public health throughout the country, according to researchers at The Ohio State University.

medical.title: Why the back? How stroking calms infants and mouse pups
medical.summary: Across cultures, the way we soothe an unsettled baby tends to look much the same: hold them close and gently rub their back. The act is so familiar that it's easy to forget how little is actually understood about why it works.

medical.title: Repurposed cancer drug rapidly eased rare autoimmune neuropathy in two patients
medical.summary: An interdisciplinary team at Jena University Hospital achieved a surprisingly rapid and significant improvement in two patients with an autoimmune peripheral nervous system disorder. The team administered teclistamab, a drug originally developed for multiple myeloma, and reported in detail in Nature Communications.

medical.title: Resetting psoriasis memory may help body stop repeat flare-ups
medical.summary: Stress, bad weather, strep throat, alcohol or cuts—there are a number of triggers that can set off psoriasis. At the same time, a great deal is happening in research on this skin disease, and new ways of controlling the immune system are being discovered.

medical.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
medical.summary: A study of long-lived families has identified rare genetic variants that may help people stay healthier for much longer as they age. One standout mutation appears to temper inflammation, potentially delaying disease and extending years of healthy living.

science.title: Astronomers spot an extremely rare galaxy mega-merger
science.summary: Scale in the universe is hard to understand from a purely human perspective. Many times, the math just doesn't sit well with our brains, which evolved to capture and process data about the world around us rather than grok the complexities of stellar dynamics and galaxy mergers. But every once in a while, astronomers find something that, if we can wrap our heads around the numbers, gives a sense of just how big the universe is.

science.title: Early parenting shapes the brain and socio-sexual behavior, rodent study shows
science.summary: Past psychology studies have consistently highlighted the importance of social bonds for survival, showing that enduring relationships are linked with a longer life expectancy, a more resilient immune system, better cardiovascular health and a lower risk of psychiatric conditions. In addition, they showed that being raised by two parents plays an important role in development, as it is often associated with better emotional regulation, cognitive performance and social competence in adulthood.

science.title: Hidden fungal networks deliver carbon to green plants, experiment confirms
science.summary: Beneath forests, grasslands and wetlands lies a hidden underground network of fungi known as mycorrhizal networks, sometimes nicknamed the "wood-wide web." These fungi live in partnership with plant roots, helping plants absorb nutrients from the soil in exchange for carbon compounds produced through photosynthesis. These fungal connections can also link different neighboring plant species.

science.title: Satellites reveal when toxic algal blooms flare in Blue Mesa Reservoir
science.summary: The summers of 2021 and 2022 were tough seasons for Colorado's Blue Mesa Reservoir. A severe drought gripped much of the western U.S., prompting emergency water releases that brought the reservoir to its lowest level since 1984. Marinas and boat ramps closed, remnants of a ghost town emerged from the muck, and parts of the reservoir turned greenish and swirled with toxic cyanobacteria blooms.

science.title: Discrepancies in AI lunar crater catalogs discovered
science.summary: A new Southwest Research Institute-led study compared eight AI-generated lunar crater catalogs, discovering that many of their published performance metrics drop sharply when the databases are evaluated using the same scientific standards humans are held to. Crater catalogs provide a comprehensive record of impact craters on planetary surfaces. They log the precise location, dimensions and physical characteristics of impact structures to help scientists understand the geological history of the solar system and its components.

science.title: Childhood trauma may erode adult relationships through daily communication struggles
science.summary: Traumatic events from your childhood could have a lingering impact on your adult relationships, according to new research from the University of Georgia.

science.title: Universities must rethink how they prepare students for an AI-powered world, study argues
science.summary: Universities need to rethink how they teach, assess and prepare students for employment as artificial intelligence becomes an increasingly important part of everyday life and work, according to a new study from the University of Manchester. The paper, published in Frontiers in Education, argues that AI is changing how people learn, work and make decisions, and that universities need to adapt to this new reality.

science.title: Why Antarctica froze millions of years before the Arctic
science.summary: East Antarctica hosts the largest ice sheet on Earth, containing enough water to raise global sea levels by 52 meters (171 feet) were it to fully melt. Yet scientists have been puzzled for decades about how and why this ice sheet formed.

science.title: Nanobubbles cleaned up the Lincoln reflecting pool: Here's how they could be used on dying seas and lakes
science.summary: Ahead of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence in the U.S., an ozone nanobubble system has been used to keep the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool clear. Months before the celebrations, a massive cleanup of the pool had taken place, but despite this, an algae bloom had turned the water bright green. To deal with this, a US$1.7 million (£1.27 million) ozone "nanobubbler" injected microscopic bubbles into the pool.

science.title: New approach boosts microplastic removal from wastewater
science.summary: RMIT University researchers have tested a more effective way to capture microplastics from wastewater, using a combination of microbubbles and nanobubbles to achieve removal rates of more than 90%.

science.title: NASA Seeks Industry Input on Second Phase of Commercial Space Stations
science.summary: On Monday, NASA released a draft Request for Proposals (RFP) seeking feedback from American companies on the next phase of its commercial space stations strategy, aimed at ensuring a seamless transition of activities in low Earth orbit from the International Space Station.  “NASA’s review reflects what we’ve been hearing from industry throughout this process. Industry […]

science.title: A Super El Niño is coming: 5 hard‑won lessons the world can learn from Africa
science.summary: Climate prediction scientists announced in June 2026 that El Niño, a cycle that happens every two to seven years, had formed. It was expected to develop into one of the strongest on record—a "super" El Niño.

science.title: Larger brain, smaller face: Human evolution took a different course than previously thought
science.summary: A new study, published July 6, 2026, in the journal Nature Communications, suggests that two of the best-known trends in human evolution—brain growth and the reduction in the size of the face and jaw—may be far less attributable to directed natural selection than scientists have long assumed. Instead, the findings by researchers at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville and the Senckenberg Centre for Human Evolution and Palaeoenvironment (SHEP) at the University of Tübingen point to a slower and presumably more limited evolutionary process than the traditional textbook portrayal suggests.

science.title: Japan releases snowman-like asteroid image after flyby
science.summary: Rare images taken by a Japanese space probe during a flyby of a near-Earth asteroid have revealed that the space rock resembled a snowman, scientists said Monday.

science.title: NASA’s CAPSTONE Completes Extended Mission Testing Lunar Technologies
science.summary: As NASA prepares for a sustained human presence on the Moon, missions will increasingly require spacecraft that can navigate and communicate without a direct connection to Earth. NASA’s Cislunar Autonomous Positioning System Technology Operations and Navigation Experiment, or CAPSTONE, validated and advanced these capabilities. Designed to test and validate technologies in lunar orbit, CAPSTONE launched […]

science.title: Q&A: How camera-equipped homing pigeons could improve robotic vision in flight
science.summary: Contrary to common assumptions, pigeons do not lock their eyes in place during flight. Instead, they make slow, subtle eye movements that may help them gather more information about their surroundings.

science.title: Engineers solved an airflow mystery hidden nearly a mile underground
science.summary: Engineers at a deep underground research facility noticed something strange during major rainstorms: airflow underground sometimes reversed direction. Using new sensors and mathematical modeling, they found that water rushing down a shaft was effectively pushing air through the tunnels like a giant piston. The breakthrough explains a long-standing mystery and could help underground operations better predict and manage ventilation during storms and emergencies.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: 😂 Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis
mix_items.summary: Distracţie mare la sediul central DIICOT din Capitală! Potrivit unor surse din interior, încă un procuror proaspăt angajat a „comis-o” şi a ajuns de râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis. „A sărit ca ars! Să fi văzut ce faţă a făcut! Vaiii, trebuia să-l filmez”, a mai transmis, printre […] The post Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Delia, Eduard Novak și Towns Up Forum completează programul Zilele Nordului 2026
mix_items.summary: Asociația Nord anunță al doilea val de noutăți și evenimente pentru cea de-a XIII-a ediție a festivalului Zilele Nordului, care va transforma orașul Darabani în epicentrul cultural al regiunii celor… Articolul Delia, Eduard Novak și Towns Up Forum completează programul Zilele Nordului 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Mai plutește deasupra Sibiului norul de aplauze de la Festival. Felicităm spectatorii de pe Scena Digitală pentru că s-a triplat numărul lor la FITS 2026! De aceea, propunem întâlniri cu… Articolul Scena Digitală propune în luna iulie două întâlniri cu emoția: Broadway și o poveste despre iubire, familie și acceptare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Biomarker-guided care to improve antidepressant response rates and reduce trial-and-error prescribing
mix_items.summary: For millions of people living with depression, finding an effective treatment can feel like a long and uncertain journey. Patients often spend months trying different medications, enduring side effects and persistent symptoms while waiting to discover whether a prescription will help. A new study led by researchers at the University of California, Irvine, and Mass General Brigham-affiliated McLean Hospital suggests there may be a better way.

mix_items.title: Why the first trimester of a pregnancy could be vital in driving human brain evolution
mix_items.summary: A new study has examined how mothers influence the size of their child's head—and, as a result, brain size and future IQ—during the first trimester of pregnancy.

mix_items.title: Astronomers spot an extremely rare galaxy mega-merger
mix_items.summary: Scale in the universe is hard to understand from a purely human perspective. Many times, the math just doesn't sit well with our brains, which evolved to capture and process data about the world around us rather than grok the complexities of stellar dynamics and galaxy mergers. But every once in a while, astronomers find something that, if we can wrap our heads around the numbers, gives a sense of just how big the universe is.

mix_items.title: Study reveals a distinct subtype of eosinophilic esophagitis
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago identified a distinct subtype of eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)—a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder in which the esophagus narrows, interfering with food passing properly. They found that 25% of children with EoE have hypermobile joints, meaning their joints can bend beyond the normal range of motion. These patients also tend to experience autonomic symptoms, most commonly chronic lightheadedness upon standing. This combination of symptoms leads to a significantly worse quality of life, according to the study published in the Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology.

mix_items.title: Early parenting shapes the brain and socio-sexual behavior, rodent study shows
mix_items.summary: Past psychology studies have consistently highlighted the importance of social bonds for survival, showing that enduring relationships are linked with a longer life expectancy, a more resilient immune system, better cardiovascular health and a lower risk of psychiatric conditions. In addition, they showed that being raised by two parents plays an important role in development, as it is often associated with better emotional regulation, cognitive performance and social competence in adulthood.

mix_items.title: Hidden fungal networks deliver carbon to green plants, experiment confirms
mix_items.summary: Beneath forests, grasslands and wetlands lies a hidden underground network of fungi known as mycorrhizal networks, sometimes nicknamed the "wood-wide web." These fungi live in partnership with plant roots, helping plants absorb nutrients from the soil in exchange for carbon compounds produced through photosynthesis. These fungal connections can also link different neighboring plant species.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why exercise reverses muscle aging
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a molecular “switch” that helps explain why exercise keeps ageing muscles healthy. By reducing levels of a gene called DEAF1, physical activity allows older muscles to clear out damage, repair themselves, and maintain strength.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover treatable cause of severe anemia associated with a cancer therapy
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Mount Sinai Tisch Cancer Center have identified a previously underrecognized folate deficiency that may cause severe anemia in some patients receiving PARP inhibitors, a widely used class of targeted cancer therapies. Their findings, published in the latest issue of the journal Blood Red Cells & Iron, suggest that routine screening and treatment with folic acid could help many patients avoid blood transfusions, hospitalizations and interruptions to their cancer treatment.

mix_items.title: Satellites reveal when toxic algal blooms flare in Blue Mesa Reservoir
mix_items.summary: The summers of 2021 and 2022 were tough seasons for Colorado's Blue Mesa Reservoir. A severe drought gripped much of the western U.S., prompting emergency water releases that brought the reservoir to its lowest level since 1984. Marinas and boat ramps closed, remnants of a ghost town emerged from the muck, and parts of the reservoir turned greenish and swirled with toxic cyanobacteria blooms.

mix_items.title: Scientists solve a 30-year rye pollen mystery that could transform cancer research
mix_items.summary: Scientists have finally solved a nearly 30-year-old mystery surrounding two unusual molecules found in rye pollen that once showed an intriguing ability to help animals fight tumors. By determining their exact 3D structures, researchers have unlocked the blueprint needed to investigate how these natural compounds interact with the immune system and which parts may be responsible for their cancer-fighting effects.

mix_items.title: Discrepancies in AI lunar crater catalogs discovered
mix_items.summary: A new Southwest Research Institute-led study compared eight AI-generated lunar crater catalogs, discovering that many of their published performance metrics drop sharply when the databases are evaluated using the same scientific standards humans are held to. Crater catalogs provide a comprehensive record of impact craters on planetary surfaces. They log the precise location, dimensions and physical characteristics of impact structures to help scientists understand the geological history of the solar system and its components.

mix_items.title: Men may be more likely than women to receive later-stage diagnoses for many cancer types in the US
mix_items.summary: Between 2015 and 2022, men were more likely than women to be diagnosed with regional and/or distant stages of 20 nonreproductive solid cancer types in the United States, according to a study published in Cancer Epidemiology, Biomarkers & Prevention, a journal of the American Association for Cancer Research (AACR).

mix_items.title: Childhood trauma may erode adult relationships through daily communication struggles
mix_items.summary: Traumatic events from your childhood could have a lingering impact on your adult relationships, according to new research from the University of Georgia.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover molecular 'switch' that simultaneously manages touch and pain
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Prof. Hyosang Lee of the Department of Brain Sciences at DGIST has identified TWIK-1, a type of potassium channel expressed in the spinal cord and peripheral sensory neurons of the somatosensory system, as a key molecular regulator of touch sensation and chronic pain. The study is significant because it presents a new molecular mechanism in the spinal cord that regulates touch responses and reveals the cause of persistent neuropathic pain, an intractable disorder, as well as a therapeutic target. The paper is published in the journal Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy.

mix_items.title: Universities must rethink how they prepare students for an AI-powered world, study argues
mix_items.summary: Universities need to rethink how they teach, assess and prepare students for employment as artificial intelligence becomes an increasingly important part of everyday life and work, according to a new study from the University of Manchester. The paper, published in Frontiers in Education, argues that AI is changing how people learn, work and make decisions, and that universities need to adapt to this new reality.

mix_items.title: Why Antarctica froze millions of years before the Arctic
mix_items.summary: East Antarctica hosts the largest ice sheet on Earth, containing enough water to raise global sea levels by 52 meters (171 feet) were it to fully melt. Yet scientists have been puzzled for decades about how and why this ice sheet formed.

mix_items.title: Brain tumor vaccine links mutation targeting to eight-year survival gains
mix_items.summary: A novel vaccination strategy against certain malignant brain tumors could fundamentally improve treatment for patients. Researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ), Mannheim University Medical Center, Heidelberg University Hospital and numerous partner institutions have published encouraging long-term results from a clinical trial involving a vaccine that activates the immune system against a common genetic mutation in these tumors.

mix_items.title: Nanobubbles cleaned up the Lincoln reflecting pool: Here's how they could be used on dying seas and lakes
mix_items.summary: Ahead of the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence in the U.S., an ozone nanobubble system has been used to keep the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool clear. Months before the celebrations, a massive cleanup of the pool had taken place, but despite this, an algae bloom had turned the water bright green. To deal with this, a US$1.7 million (£1.27 million) ozone "nanobubbler" injected microscopic bubbles into the pool.

mix_items.title: New approach boosts microplastic removal from wastewater
mix_items.summary: RMIT University researchers have tested a more effective way to capture microplastics from wastewater, using a combination of microbubbles and nanobubbles to achieve removal rates of more than 90%.

mix_items.title: Violence may bring menopause up to 20 months earlier, review suggests
mix_items.summary: A study by the Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology at the University of Granada has revealed that exposure to violence against women throughout life has lasting effects that extend into middle age. The research, published in Maturitas, shows that women who have experienced violence suffer more severe menopausal symptoms and may reach menopause up to 20 months earlier than those without such a history. The study notes that for every woman who dies as a result of violence, more than 400 suffer serious disabilities, the aftereffects of which are often confused with or exacerbated by menopause.

mix_items.title: NASA Seeks Industry Input on Second Phase of Commercial Space Stations
mix_items.summary: On Monday, NASA released a draft Request for Proposals (RFP) seeking feedback from American companies on the next phase of its commercial space stations strategy, aimed at ensuring a seamless transition of activities in low Earth orbit from the International Space Station.  “NASA’s review reflects what we’ve been hearing from industry throughout this process. Industry […]

mix_items.title: A Super El Niño is coming: 5 hard‑won lessons the world can learn from Africa
mix_items.summary: Climate prediction scientists announced in June 2026 that El Niño, a cycle that happens every two to seven years, had formed. It was expected to develop into one of the strongest on record—a "super" El Niño.

mix_items.title: Larger brain, smaller face: Human evolution took a different course than previously thought
mix_items.summary: A new study, published July 6, 2026, in the journal Nature Communications, suggests that two of the best-known trends in human evolution—brain growth and the reduction in the size of the face and jaw—may be far less attributable to directed natural selection than scientists have long assumed. Instead, the findings by researchers at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville and the Senckenberg Centre for Human Evolution and Palaeoenvironment (SHEP) at the University of Tübingen point to a slower and presumably more limited evolutionary process than the traditional textbook portrayal suggests.

mix_items.title: Social media affects teen dental confidence
mix_items.summary: Using social media can have a negative impact on adolescents' confidence about the appearance of their teeth, new research shows. University of Otago–Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka researchers have co-authored a paper, published in Health Marketing Quarterly, that recommends policymakers move away from idealized smile imagery in oral health marketing.

mix_items.title: Paying stores to curb tobacco, alcohol and lottery ads shows early promise
mix_items.summary: An initiative focused on partnering with retailers to decrease the influence of tobacco, alcohol and lottery marketing is showing promise and could present a more effective way to protect public health throughout the country, according to researchers at The Ohio State University.

mix_items.title: Japan releases snowman-like asteroid image after flyby
mix_items.summary: Rare images taken by a Japanese space probe during a flyby of a near-Earth asteroid have revealed that the space rock resembled a snowman, scientists said Monday.

mix_items.title: Why the back? How stroking calms infants and mouse pups
mix_items.summary: Across cultures, the way we soothe an unsettled baby tends to look much the same: hold them close and gently rub their back. The act is so familiar that it's easy to forget how little is actually understood about why it works.

mix_items.title: NASA’s CAPSTONE Completes Extended Mission Testing Lunar Technologies
mix_items.summary: As NASA prepares for a sustained human presence on the Moon, missions will increasingly require spacecraft that can navigate and communicate without a direct connection to Earth. NASA’s Cislunar Autonomous Positioning System Technology Operations and Navigation Experiment, or CAPSTONE, validated and advanced these capabilities. Designed to test and validate technologies in lunar orbit, CAPSTONE launched […]

mix_items.title: Q&A: How camera-equipped homing pigeons could improve robotic vision in flight
mix_items.summary: Contrary to common assumptions, pigeons do not lock their eyes in place during flight. Instead, they make slow, subtle eye movements that may help them gather more information about their surroundings.

mix_items.title: Repurposed cancer drug rapidly eased rare autoimmune neuropathy in two patients
mix_items.summary: An interdisciplinary team at Jena University Hospital achieved a surprisingly rapid and significant improvement in two patients with an autoimmune peripheral nervous system disorder. The team administered teclistamab, a drug originally developed for multiple myeloma, and reported in detail in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Resetting psoriasis memory may help body stop repeat flare-ups
mix_items.summary: Stress, bad weather, strep throat, alcohol or cuts—there are a number of triggers that can set off psoriasis. At the same time, a great deal is happening in research on this skin disease, and new ways of controlling the immune system are being discovered.

mix_items.title: Engineers solved an airflow mystery hidden nearly a mile underground
mix_items.summary: Engineers at a deep underground research facility noticed something strange during major rainstorms: airflow underground sometimes reversed direction. Using new sensors and mathematical modeling, they found that water rushing down a shaft was effectively pushing air through the tunnels like a giant piston. The breakthrough explains a long-standing mystery and could help underground operations better predict and manage ventilation during storms and emergencies.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
mix_items.summary: A study of long-lived families has identified rare genetic variants that may help people stay healthier for much longer as they age. One standout mutation appears to temper inflammation, potentially delaying disease and extending years of healthy living.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am vrut sa dorm devreme, dar gandurile aveau program prelungit.

satire_ro.title: Un procuror DIICOT a stârnit râsul colegilor după ce a pus mâna din greşeală pe dosarul Nordis
